original_hypothesis.title: Minting can be halted if collection randomizer is unset/EOA: _mintProcessing calls randomizer.calculateTokenHash and will revert
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: NextGenCore._mintProcessing unconditionally calls `collectionAdditionalData[_collectionID].randomizer.calculateTokenHash(...)`. If a collection's randomizer is not configured, points to address(0), or points to a contract not implementing the interface, the external call will revert and all mints/airdrops/burnToMint for that collection will fail.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a hard dependency in the minting critical path. Even if intended, it is a common production failure mode (misconfiguration, accidental reset, wrong contract). It is exploitable as a griefing vector if an attacker can influence configuration (or if admins mistakenly set it).
deployment_assumptions: A collection is created and made mintable without a valid randomizerContract/randomizer set
Or admin sets randomizerContract to an EOA/incorrect contract
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: fixturesDeployment.js deploys randomizer but does not show any call to NextGenCore.addRandomizer(...) or addMinterContract(...); no production deployment evidence provided.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unconditional external call `collectionAdditionalData[_collectionID].randomizer.calculateTokenHash(...)` without checking randomizer address != 0

preconditions: No try/catch to tolerate randomizer failure

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: complete mint DoS for affected collection
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(randomizerContract != address(0)); no fallback randomness mode

analysis_questions: Is there any enforced setup ordering ensuring addRandomizer is always called before enabling minting phases in the minter?
Can addRandomizer be called per-collection after mint starts, and what happens to already-minted tokens pending hashes?
Could an incorrect randomizer cause partial state changes (e.g., if supply increments occur before randomizer call)?
validation_requirements: Test mint with randomizer unset (address(0)) and confirm revert
Test admin setting randomizer to a contract that reverts and confirm mint DoS
Check interactions with the phantom supply bug (TECH-H001) when reverts occur

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a collection is launched without a randomizer properly configured, every mint transaction reverts. Users cannot mint and sale is effectively halted until admins fix configuration.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration or admin mistake (or attacker gains admin privileges off-chain)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High: halts minting and revenue for impacted collections
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting to mint that collection
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High if admins can set correct randomizer promptly

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing tests for unset randomizer and setup sequencing

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: during collection launch or after admin updates
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor addRandomizer events (none exist) and randomizerContract storage values per collection

validation_result.mechanism: Missing randomizer prerequisite check in mint critical path
validation_result.impact_summary: If a collection's randomizer is never set via addRandomizer(), all mint/airdrop/burnToMint calls for that collection will revert because _mintProcessing unconditionally calls calculateTokenHash on address(0).
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _mintProcessing function in NextGenCore.sol unconditionally invokes collectionAdditionalData[_collectionID].randomizer.calculateTokenHash(...). The randomizer field is only populated when an admin explicitly calls addRandomizer(). There is no enforced ordering that requires addRandomizer() to be called before minting phases can be configured and activated. The setup chain is: createCollection → setCollectionData → setCollectionCosts → setCollectionPhases → mint(). None of these steps require that addRandomizer() has been called. If the randomizer is never set, it defaults to IRandomizer(address(0)), and any call to calculateTokenHash on address(0) will revert (no code at that address). This creates a complete DoS for minting on the affected collection. The addRandomizer function itself does validate the target contract, so the randomizer cannot be set to an EOA or non-implementing contract through that function. The defect is specifically the lack of enforcement that addRandomizer must be called before minting can proceed.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies _mintProcessing as the location. Line-by-line tracing confirms the unconditional external call to randomizer.calculateTokenHash without any guard for address(0). The deployment script (fixturesDeployment.js) deploys the randomizer contract but never calls addRandomizer or addMinterContract, confirming these are separate manual steps with no enforced ordering.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Unconditionally calls randomizer.calculateTokenHash without checking that the randomizer address is non-zero. If addRandomizer was never called for this collection, this calls calculateTokenHash on address(0), which reverts.

Calls _mintProcessing which calls unset randomizer. Note: collectionCirculationSupply is incremented BEFORE _mintProcessing, so a revert here still rolls back the increment (Solidity 0.8 revert behavior). However, this means the entire airdrop transaction fails.
Only checks wereDataAdded (set by setCollectionData), NOT whether addRandomizer has been called. This allows minting costs and phases to be fully configured without a randomizer.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls MinterContract.mint() with valid phase timing, merkle proof, and ETH
2. mint() passes all checks: setMintingCosts, time window, supply, msg.value
3. mint() calls gencore.mint(mintIndex, mintingAddress, _mintTo, tokData, _saltfun_o, col, phase)
8. randomizer is IRandomizer(address(0)) → REVERT: no code at address(0)
9. Entire transaction reverts, including supply increment
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: _mintProcessing is called by all three mint functions (airDropTokens, mint, burnToMint). Each of these is called from MinterContract. The critical path is: MinterContract.mint → NextGenCore.mint → _mintProcessing → randomizer.calculateTokenHash. The randomizer field defaults to address(0) when no addRandomizer call has been made. The call to address(0) has no code to execute, causing EVM revert. No try/catch is used, so the revert propagates up to the user's transaction.

deployment_assumptions_checked: A collection is created and made mintable without a valid randomizerContract/randomizer set
Or admin sets randomizerContract to an EOA/incorrect contract
config_validation.deployment_evidence: fixturesDeployment.js deploys NextGenRandomizerNXT but does NOT call hhCore.addRandomizer(collectionId, randomizerAddress) or hhCore.addMinterContract(minterAddress). These are separate setup steps not enforced by the deployment script. The test fixture only creates contracts, not the full configuration chain.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is straightforward: if an admin creates a collection, sets collection data, sets minting costs and phases, but forgets to call addRandomizer(), all mint transactions will revert. This is a realistic misconfiguration scenario. The second assumption (admin setting randomizer to EOA) is NOT possible through addRandomizer() since it validates isRandomizerContract(), but the first assumption (never calling addRandomizer) is entirely possible since no step in the configuration chain enforces it. The defect is real regardless of whether it can be externally exploited - it's a missing prerequisite check.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact for affected collections. All minting operations (mint, airDropTokens, burnToMint, mintAndAuction) are blocked until an admin calls addRandomizer(). Users' transactions will revert, wasting gas. If this occurs during a time-limited sale (salesOption 2 with descending price), the collection could miss its minting window entirely. Recovery is possible by calling addRandomizer() with a valid randomizer contract.
recommended_mitigations: Add require(address(collectionAdditionalData[_collectionID].randomizer) != address(0), 'Randomizer not set') in _mintProcessing before calling calculateTokenHash
Add a check in MinterContract's mint paths (or in setCollectionPhases) that verifies the randomizer is configured before allowing minting to be activated
Create a setup validation function that checks all prerequisites (minterContract, randomizer, collection data) are configured before minting phases can begin
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts via fixturesDeployment. 2. Call createCollection and setCollectionData for collection 1. 3. Call addMinterContract with the minter address. 4. Call setCollectionCosts and setCollectionPhases for collection 1. 5. Do NOT call addRandomizer. 6. Attempt to call mint() during the public phase. 7. Verify the transaction reverts. 8. Call addRandomizer with a valid randomizer. 9. Verify mint() succeeds.
uncertainties: The deployment script is a test fixture, not a production deployment script - production deployment may have safeguards not visible in the test fixture
It's possible the project has off-chain tooling or checklists that enforce the setup ordering, but this is not enforced on-chain
additional_testing_needed: Verify that calling _mintProcessing with unset randomizer actually reverts (not just returns empty data)

Test the interaction with the phantom supply bug: if circulationSupply is incremented before _mintProcessing in some code path but the revert rolls it back, confirm no permanent state corruption occurs
Test that addRandomizer properly prevents setting EOA addresses
